behaviors.description: The clause extractor identifies all key terms (parties, dates, obligations, penalties) present in the contract
behaviors.justification: This behavior depends fundamentally on what the real LLM can extract. A mock would let you test 'if the LLM returns structured data, does the parser work?' but would hide failures where: (a) the prompt doesn't elicit the right format or completeness from a real model, (b) the model simply doesn't recognize key terms the domain expects, or (c) the prompt engineering is inadequate for the real model's capabilities. Only a real LLM call reveals whether the clause extractor actually finds all terms present.
behaviors.test_description: Run the clause extractor against a real contract (or test set of contracts) with a real LLM backend. Verify that all ground-truth key terms (parties, dates, obligations, penalties) are extracted. Use contracts with diverse phrasing to catch prompt brittleness.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Agentic is correct. Unit/Integration mocks would pass even if the real model fails silently. The core risk—'model doesn't extract complete terms'—is only visible at Agentic level.

behaviors.description: The risk assessor flags language that would be problematic under the configured risk policy, even when the problematic phrasing is indirect or euphemistic
behaviors.justification: The explicit mention of 'indirect or euphemistic' phrasing is the key. A mock would let you test 'if I tell the LLM to flag risk X, does it output the right JSON?' But mocking hides the core failure: 'does the real LLM actually detect euphemistic risk language in the wild?' This is a capability test, not a format test. Only running against a real LLM with real policy config reveals whether it catches subtle, obfuscated risk.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test corpus of contracts containing indirect/euphemistic risk language (e.g., 'may limit our exposure to technical improvements' instead of 'liability cap'). Run the risk assessor against real contracts with various policy configurations. Verify all euphemistic risks are flagged correctly.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Agentic is necessary. Integration tests with mocked LLM outputs would pass even if the real model misses 90% of indirect language. The human judgment of 'is this phrasing actually risky?' is what makes this Agentic.

behaviors.description: The compliance checker correctly determines whether the contract meets all applicable regulatory requirements for the configured jurisdiction
behaviors.justification: Correctness here depends entirely on what the real LLM knows about regulations for a specific jurisdiction. A mock would test 'if I tell the model regulatory requirement X, does it recognize it?' but would hide failures where: (a) the model's regulatory knowledge is outdated or incomplete, (b) the jurisdiction config doesn't map to rules the model understands, (c) the model confuses similar requirements across jurisdictions. Only a real LLM call exposes whether compliance checks are actually accurate.
behaviors.test_description: For each supported jurisdiction, test the compliance checker against contracts that are known to violate/satisfy specific regulatory requirements (sourced from legal review). Verify correct pass/fail decisions. Include edge cases like contracts meeting base requirements but missing newer amendments.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Agentic is correct. Regulatory correctness is a knowledge/judgment call, not a logic problem. Mocking would hide gaps in what the model knows.

behaviors.description: When two reviewers flag the same clause with conflicting assessments (e.g., extractor says 'standard' but risk assessor says 'high risk'), the merge step preserves both assessments with attribution
behaviors.justification: The merge logic itself—preserving both conflicting outputs with attribution—is a deterministic, data-structure problem. You can test this with mocked reviewer outputs (fixed conflict scenarios) and verify the merge produces the correct unified report structure. However, this does NOT test whether conflicts actually occur in practice or whether the data structures from real reviewers match what the merge expects.
behaviors.test_description: Unit/Integration: Mock three reviewers with pre-set conflicting outputs (e.g., extractor labels a clause 'standard', risk assessor labels it 'high risk'). Verify merge produces a report with both assessments, each attributed to its source. Test multiple conflict patterns: same clause, different risk scores; same clause, different compliance verdicts; overlapping vs. disjoint clause boundaries.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Integration tests the merge logic in isolation, but misses: (1) Do real LLM reviewers actually produce conflicts at expected frequencies? (2) Do real reviewer outputs match the data structure the merge expects? (3) Can the merge correctly identify 'same clause' when the three reviewers use slightly different identifiers? A Workflow test is needed to verify conflicts occur and are handled end-to-end.

behaviors.description: The full pipeline — dispatching to all three reviewers, collecting results, merging into a unified report — completes correctly for a real contract without losing any individual reviewer's findings
behaviors.justification: This requires all three reviewers running in parallel on a real contract, collecting their outputs, and merging them into a unified report. The key phrase 'without losing any findings' means you must verify that each reviewer's output is accounted for in the final report. This can only be tested by running the real pipeline end-to-end with a real contract (or high-fidelity test contract). Any level below Workflow would mock one or more reviewers, potentially hiding: failures in parallel coordination, lost outputs due to race conditions, or merge logic that silently drops findings.
behaviors.test_description: Workflow test: Run the full pipeline on a real contract (or test contracts with ground truth). Verify: (1) All three reviewers complete and produce output, (2) The unified report contains all findings from all three reviewers, (3) No findings are dropped or duplicated in the merge, (4) Attribution and conflict resolution are applied correctly. Test with contracts that produce high volumes of findings to stress the merge logic.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Workflow is necessary and sufficient. Any lower level (even System with mocked reviewers) risks hiding real failures in parallel execution, output collection, or merge correctness. The end-to-end contract processing is what must not lose data.
